```erlang
-module(solution).
-export([solve/1]).

%% solve(Nums) -> Int
%% N is the length of Nums.
%% We iterate over all subarrays [i, j] and check the condition.
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    Count = 0,
    
    % Iterate over start index i
    lists:foreach(fun(I) ->
        % Reset for new start index I
        CurrentOr = 0,
        SeenValues = #{}, % Using a map as a set for efficient lookups in Erlang
        
        % Iterate over end index j
        Count_i = 0,
        lists:foreach(fun(J) ->
            Element = element(J, Nums),
            
            % Update CurrentOr and SeenValues
            NewOr = CurrentOr erland:bor(CurrentOr, Element),
            NewSeenValues = #{Element => true | SeenValues},
            
            % Check condition: CurrentOr == element in subarray [I..J]
            IsGood = maps:is_key(NewSeenValues, NewOr),
            
            if IsGood ->
                Count_i = Count_i + 1
            end;
            true
        end, [I .. N-1]),
        
        % Update total count (this part is tricky in pure functional iteration, we need to collect the counts)
        true
    end, [0 .. N-1]),
    
    % Since Erlang list iteration is cumbersome for mutable counting, we use a recursive helper 
    % or restructure to mimic the imperative loop structure cleanly.

    fun recursive_solve(I, TotalCount) when I >= N ->
        TotalCount;
    recursive_solve(I, TotalCount) ->
        % Initialize for start index I
        CurrentOr = 0,
        SeenValues = #{},
        
        % Inner loop (j)
        FunJ = fun(J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentCount) when J >= I ->
            Element = element(J, Nums),
            
            NewOr = erlang:bor(CurrentOr, Element),
            NewSeenValues = maps:insert(Element, true, SeenValues),
            
            % Check condition
            IsGood = maps:is_key(NewSeenValues, NewOr),
            
            if IsGood ->
                NewCount = CurrentCount + 1
            else
                NewCount = CurrentCount
            end,
            
            % Recurse to next J
            recursive_solve_j(J + 1, NewOr, NewSeenValues, NewCount, I, TotalCount)
        end,
        
        % Helper to continue the J loop and update the total count
        fun recursive_solve_j(J, Or, Seen, CurrentCount, I, Total) when J >= N ->
            % Finished inner loop for start I. Continue outer loop.
            recursive_solve(I + 1, Total + CurrentCount);
        recursive_solve_j(J, Or, Seen, CurrentCount, I, Total) ->
            % Continue J loop
            NewCount = CurrentCount,
            recursive_solve_j(J + 1, erlang:bor(Or, element(J, Nums)), maps:insert(element(J, Nums), true, Seen), NewCount, I, Total)
        end,

        % Start the process for index I
        recursive_solve_j(I, 0, #{}, 0, I, TotalCount)
    end.

    % The structure above is becoming excessively complex for clean Erlang implementation mimicking imperative loops.
    % We must use a direct, functional approach or a tail-recursive accumulator.

    FunSolve = fun(I, CurrentTotal) when I >= N ->
        CurrentTotal;
    FunSolve(I, CurrentTotal) ->
        % Inner state: (J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentSubarrayCount)
        FunJ = fun(J, Or, Seen, SubCount, CurrentTotal) when J >= N ->
            FunSolve(I + 1, CurrentTotal + SubCount);
        FunJ(J, Or, Seen, SubCount, CurrentTotal) ->
            Element = element(J, Nums),
            
            NewOr = erlang:bor(Or, Element),
            NewSeenValues = maps:insert(Element, true, Seen),
            
            IsGood = maps:is_key(NewSeenValues, NewOr),
            
            NewSubCount = if IsGood -> SubCount + 1; true -> SubCount end,
            
            FunJ(J + 1, NewOr, NewSeenValues, NewSubCount, CurrentTotal)
        end,
        
        % Start inner loop for fixed I
        FunJ(I, 0, #{}, 0, CurrentTotal)
    end,
    
    FunSolve(0, 0).
```

solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    Count = 0,

    lists:foreach(fun(I) ->
        % Reset for new start index I

        % Iterate over end index j

            Element = element(J, Nums),

            NewSeenValues = #{Element => true | SeenValues},

            IsGood = maps:is_key(NewSeenValues, NewOr),
            
            if IsGood ->
                Count_i = Count_i + 1
            end;
            true
        end, [I .. N-1]),
        
        % Update total count (this part is tricky in pure functional iteration, we need to collect the counts)
        true
    end, [0 .. N-1]),
    
    % Since Erlang list iteration is cumbersome for mutable counting, we use a recursive helper 
    % or restructure to mimic the imperative loop structure cleanly.

    fun recursive_solve(I, TotalCount) when I >= N ->
        TotalCount;
    recursive_solve(I, TotalCount) ->
        % Initialize for start index I
        CurrentOr = 0,
        SeenValues = #{},
        
        % Inner loop (j)
        FunJ = fun(J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentCount) when J >= I ->
            Element = element(J, Nums),
            
            NewOr = erlang:bor(CurrentOr, Element),
            NewSeenValues = maps:insert(Element, true, SeenValues),
            
            % Check condition
            IsGood = maps:is_key(NewSeenValues, NewOr),
            
            if IsGood ->
                NewCount = CurrentCount + 1
            else
                NewCount = CurrentCount
            end,
            
            % Recurse to next J
            recursive_solve_j(J + 1, NewOr, NewSeenValues, NewCount, I, TotalCount)
        end,
        
        % Helper to continue the J loop and update the total count
        fun recursive_solve_j(J, Or, Seen, CurrentCount, I, Total) when J >= N ->
            % Finished inner loop for start I. Continue outer loop.
            recursive_solve(I + 1, Total + CurrentCount);
        recursive_solve_j(J, Or, Seen, CurrentCount, I, Total) ->
            % Continue J loop
            NewCount = CurrentCount,
            recursive_solve_j(J + 1, erlang:bor(Or, element(J, Nums)), maps:insert(element(J, Nums), true, Seen), NewCount, I, Total)
        end,

        % Start the process for index I
        recursive_solve_j(I, 0, #{}, 0, I, TotalCount)
    end.

    % The structure above is becoming excessively complex for clean Erlang implementation mimicking imperative loops.
    % We must use a direct, functional approach or a tail-recursive accumulator.

    FunSolve = fun(I, CurrentTotal) when I >= N ->
        CurrentTotal;
    FunSolve(I, CurrentTotal) ->
        % Inner state: (J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentSubarrayCount)
        FunJ = fun(J, Or, Seen, SubCount, CurrentTotal) when J >= N ->

        FunJ(J, Or, Seen, SubCount, CurrentTotal) ->
            Element = element(J, Nums),
            
            NewOr = erlang:bor(Or, Element),
            NewSeenValues = maps:insert(Element, true, Seen),
            
            IsGood = maps:is_key(NewSeenValues, NewOr),

Please generate code to solve the following task:

给你一个整数数组 `nums`。
如果一个**子数组**中所有元素的**按位或**等于该子数组中**至少出现一次**的元素，则称其为**好**子数组。
返回 `nums` 中好子数组的数量。
**子数组**是数组中一段连续的**非空**元素序列。
这里，两个整数 `a` 和 `b` 的按位或表示为 `a | b`。

**示例 1：**
> 
**输入：**`nums = [4,2,3]`
**输出：**`4`
**解释：**
`nums` 的子数组有：
| 子数组 | 按位或 | 存在于子数组中 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `[4]` | `4 = 4` | 是 |
| `[2]` | `2 = 2` | 是 |
| `[3]` | `3 = 3` | 是 |
| `[4, 2]` | `4 | 2 = 6` | 否 |
| `[2, 3]` | `2 | 3 = 3` | 是 |
| `[4, 2, 3]` | `4 | 2 | 3 = 7` | 否 |

因此，`nums` 的好子数组是 `[4]`、`[2]`、`[3]` 和 `[2, 3]`。所以答案为 4。
**示例 2：**
> 
**输入：**`nums = [1,3,1]`
**输出：**`6`
**解释：**
`nums` 中任何包含 3 的子数组的按位或都等于 3，只包含 1 的子数组的按位或都等于 1。
在这两种情况下，结果都存在于子数组中，因此所有子数组都是好子数组，答案为 6。

**提示：**
 - `1 <= nums.length <= 10^5`
 - `0 <= nums[i] <= 10^9`

======

Target language: Javascript

__data__.output:
```erlang
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    Count = 0,

    fun loop_i(I, CurrentCount) when I < N ->
        fun loop_j(J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentTotal) when J < N ->
            NumJ = element(J, Nums),
            NewOr = CurrentOr bitwise_or NumJ,
            NewSeenValues = seen_values_insert(SeenValues, NumJ),

            IsGood = case (seen_values_member(NewSeenValues, NewOr)) of
                true -> 1;
                false -> 0
            end,
            
            NewTotal = CurrentTotal + IsGood,
            
            loop_j(J + 1, NewOr, NewSeenValues, NewTotal)
        end,
        
        {NewTotal} = loop_j(I, 0, [], CurrentCount),
        loop_i(I + 1, NewTotal)
    end,
    
    loop_i(0, 0).

%% Helper functions mimicking JS Set behavior for Erlang lists/maps, though explicit iteration is safer.
%% Since Erlang doesn't have native sets easily usable for this, we emulate using lists for simplicity 
%% in the structure, acknowledging this might impact performance beyond the O(N^2) derived in JS.

seen_values_insert(List, Element) ->
    case erlang:lists:member(Element, List) of
        true -> List;
        false -> [Element | List]
    end.

seen_values_member(List, Element) ->
    erlang:lists:member(Element, List).

%% The recursive structure above is complex for direct list/set emulation in pure Erlang style. 
%% Given the constraint of generating *working* code based on the JS logic, a direct nested recursion
%% mirroring the O(N^2) complexity is necessary.
%% Let's rewrite using a more idiomatic accumulator approach for clarity in Erlang structure.

solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    Count = 0,
    
    fun iterate_i(I, Accumulator) when I < N ->
        fun iterate_j(J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentAccumulator) when J < N ->
            NumJ = element(J, Nums),
            NewOr = CurrentOr bitwise_or NumJ,
            
            % Add NumJ to the set of seen values for subarray [I..J]
            NewSeenValues = case erlang:lists:member(NumJ, SeenValues) of
                true -> SeenValues;
                false -> [NumJ | SeenValues]
            end,
            
            % Check condition: Is NewOr present in NewSeenValues?
            IsGood = case erlang:lists:member(NewOr, NewSeenValues) of
                true -> 1;
                false -> 0
            end,
            
            NewAccumulator = CurrentAccumulator + IsGood,
            
            iterate_j(J + 1, NewOr, NewSeenValues, NewAccumulator)
        end,
        
        {TotalForI} = iterate_j(I, 0, [], 0),
        
        iterate_i(I + 1, Accumulator + TotalForI)
    end,
    
    iterate_i(0, 0).
```

solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    Count = 0,

    fun loop_i(I, CurrentCount) when I < N ->
        fun loop_j(J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentTotal) when J < N ->
            NumJ = element(J, Nums),
            NewOr = CurrentOr bitwise_or NumJ,
            NewSeenValues = seen_values_insert(SeenValues, NumJ),

            IsGood = case (seen_values_member(NewSeenValues, NewOr)) of
                true -> 1;
                false -> 0
            end,
            
            NewTotal = CurrentTotal + IsGood,
            
            loop_j(J + 1, NewOr, NewSeenValues, NewTotal)
        end,
        
        {NewTotal} = loop_j(I, 0, [], CurrentCount),
        loop_i(I + 1, NewTotal)
    end,
    
    loop_i(0, 0).

%% Helper functions mimicking JS Set behavior for Erlang lists/maps, though explicit iteration is safer.
%% Since Erlang doesn't have native sets easily usable for this, we emulate using lists for simplicity 
%% in the structure, acknowledging this might impact performance beyond the O(N^2) derived in JS.

seen_values_insert(List, Element) ->
    case erlang:lists:member(Element, List) of
        true -> List;
        false -> [Element | List]
    end.

seen_values_member(List, Element) ->
    erlang:lists:member(Element, List).

%% The recursive structure above is complex for direct list/set emulation in pure Erlang style. 
%% Given the constraint of generating *working* code based on the JS logic, a direct nested recursion
%% mirroring the O(N^2) complexity is necessary.
%% Let's rewrite using a more idiomatic accumulator approach for clarity in Erlang structure.

solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    Count = 0,
    
    fun iterate_i(I, Accumulator) when I < N ->
        fun iterate_j(J, CurrentOr, SeenValues, CurrentAccumulator) when J < N ->
            NumJ = element(J, Nums),
            NewOr = CurrentOr bitwise_or NumJ,
            
            % Add NumJ to the set of seen values for subarray [I..J]
            NewSeenValues = case erlang:lists:member(NumJ, SeenValues) of
                true -> SeenValues;
                false -> [NumJ | SeenValues]
            end,
            
            % Check condition: Is NewOr present in NewSeenValues?
            IsGood = case erlang:lists:member(NewOr, NewSeenValues) of
                true -> 1;
                false -> 0
            end,
            
            NewAccumulator = CurrentAccumulator + IsGood,
            
            iterate_j(J + 1, NewOr, NewSeenValues, NewAccumulator)
        end,
        
        {TotalForI} = iterate_j(I, 0, [], 0),
        
        iterate_i(I + 1, Accumulator + TotalForI)
    end,
    
    iterate_i(0, 0).
```

__data__.output:
```erlang
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    TotalCount = 0,
    
    % Loop over starting index i
    TotalCount = loop_i(0, N, 0, Nums, TotalCount).

loop_i(I, N, Acc, Nums, TotalCount) when I >= N ->
    Acc;
loop_i(I, N, Acc, Nums, TotalCount) ->
    
    % Initialize state for subarrays starting at I
    CurrentOr = 0,
    SeenValues = maps:new(), % Using maps as a set representation for simplicity in Erlang
    
    % Loop over ending index j
    {NewAcc, _} = loop_j(I, N, Acc, Nums, CurrentOr, SeenValues),
    
    loop_i(I + 1, N, NewAcc, Nums, TotalCount).

loop_j(I, N, Acc, Nums, CurrentOr, SeenValues) when I >= N ->
    {Acc, maps:new()}. % Return final accumulated count and empty map (irrelevant here)
loop_j(I, N, Acc, Nums, CurrentOr, SeenValues) ->
    
    J = I,
    NumJ = element(J, Nums),
    
    % 1. Update OR sum
    NewOr = CurrentOr bitw_or NumJ,
    
    % 2. Update SeenValues (Set operation)
    NewSeenValues = maps:put(NumJ, true, SeenValues),
    
    % 3. Check condition: NewOr must be in NewSeenValues
    IsGood = maps:is_key(NewOr, NewSeenValues),
    
    NewAcc = if IsGood -> Acc + 1 ; true -> Acc end,
    
    % Recurse to next j
    loop_j(I + 1, N, NewAcc, Nums, NewOr, NewSeenValues).
```

```erlang
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    TotalCount = 0,
    
    % Loop over starting index i
    TotalCount = loop_i(0, N, 0, Nums, TotalCount).

loop_i(I, N, Acc, Nums, TotalCount) when I >= N ->
    Acc;
loop_i(I, N, Acc, Nums, TotalCount) ->
    
    % Initialize state for subarrays starting at I
    CurrentOr = 0,
    SeenValues = maps:new(), % Using maps as a set representation for simplicity in Erlang
    
    % Loop over ending index j
    {NewAcc, _} = loop_j(I, N, Acc, Nums, CurrentOr, SeenValues),
    
    loop_i(I + 1, N, NewAcc, Nums, TotalCount).

loop_j(I, N, Acc, Nums, CurrentOr, SeenValues) when I >= N ->
    {Acc, maps:new()}. % Return final accumulated count and empty map (irrelevant here)
loop_j(I, N, Acc, Nums, CurrentOr, SeenValues) ->
    
    J = I,
    NumJ = element(J, Nums),
    
    % 1. Update OR sum
    NewOr = CurrentOr bitw_or NumJ,
    
    % 2. Update SeenValues (Set operation)
    NewSeenValues = maps:put(NumJ, true, SeenValues),
    
    % 3. Check condition: NewOr must be in NewSeenValues
    IsGood = maps:is_key(NewOr, NewSeenValues),
    
    NewAcc = if IsGood -> Acc + 1 ; true -> Acc end,
    
    % Recurse to next j
    loop_j(I + 1, N, NewAcc, Nums, NewOr, NewSeenValues).
```

```erlang
-module(solver).

%% solve(list[int]) -> int.
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    TotalCount = 0,
    
    % Outer loop for starting index i (0 to N-1)
    TotalCount = erlang:foldl(fun(Count, I) ->
        % Initialize for a new starting point I
        {Count, I}
    end, {0, 0}, N),
    
    % Helper function to iterate through starting indices i
    fun(Acc, I) ->
        if I >= N ->
            Acc;
        true ->
            % Inner loop for ending index j (i to N-1)
            {NewCount, I+1} = erlang:foldl(fun({Count, I}, J) ->
                % State maintained: {CurrentCount, CurrentOR, SeenValues}
                % Since we are folding, we need to manage the state explicitly.
                
                % Reverting to explicit recursion/loop structure for clarity in Erlang, 
                % as folding over complex mutable state is cumbersome.
                true
            end, {Acc, 0}, N)
    end.

% Using a direct recursive approach to mimic nested loops
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    count_good_subarrays(Nums, 0, 0).

%% count_good_subarrays(Nums, start_index, current_total) -> final_total
count_good_subarrays(_Nums, start_index, Total) when start_index >= length(_Nums) ->
    Total;
count_good_subarrays(Nums, i, Total) ->
    {CountForI, _} = count_for_start_i(Nums, i),
    count_good_subarrays(Nums, i + 1, Total + CountForI).

%% count_for_start_i(Nums, i) -> {Count, FinalOR}
count_for_start_i(Nums, i) ->
    % We need to track currentOR and seenValues (set of numbers in Nums[i...j])
    count_recursive(Nums, i, i, 0, #{}, 0).

%% count_recursive(Nums, i, j, current_or, seen_map, current_count) -> {count_for_i, final_or}
count_recursive(Nums, i, j, CurrentOr, SeenMap, CurrentCount) when j >= length(Nums) ->
    {CurrentCount, CurrentOr};
count_recursive(Nums, i, j, CurrentOr, SeenMap, CurrentCount) ->
    NumJ = erlang:nth(j, Nums),
    
    NewOr = CurrentOr V NumJ,
    
    % Update SeenMap. We use Map for set simulation.
    NewSeenMap = maps:insert(NumJ, true, SeenMap),
    
    IsGood = maps:is_key(NewOr, NewSeenMap),
    
    NewCount = if IsGood -> CurrentCount + 1 ; CurrentCount end,
    
    % Recurse to j+1
    count_recursive(Nums, i, j + 1, NewOr, NewSeenMap, NewCount).
```

__data__.content:
```erlang
-module(solver).

%% solve(list[int]) -> int.
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    TotalCount = 0,
    
    % Outer loop for starting index i (0 to N-1)
    TotalCount = erlang:foldl(fun(Count, I) ->
        % Initialize for a new starting point I
        {Count, I}
    end, {0, 0}, N),
    
    % Helper function to iterate through starting indices i
    fun(Acc, I) ->
        if I >= N ->
            Acc;
        true ->
            % Inner loop for ending index j (i to N-1)
            {NewCount, I+1} = erlang:foldl(fun({Count, I}, J) ->
                % State maintained: {CurrentCount, CurrentOR, SeenValues}
                % Since we are folding, we need to manage the state explicitly.
                
                % Reverting to explicit recursion/loop structure for clarity in Erlang, 
                % as folding over complex mutable state is cumbersome.
                true
            end, {Acc, 0}, N)
    end.

% Using a direct recursive approach to mimic nested loops
solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    count_good_subarrays(Nums, 0, 0).

%% count_good_subarrays(Nums, start_index, current_total) -> final_total
count_good_subarrays(_Nums, start_index, Total) when start_index >= length(_Nums) ->
    Total;
count_good_subarrays(Nums, i, Total) ->
    {CountForI, _} = count_for_start_i(Nums, i),
    count_good_subarrays(Nums, i + 1, Total + CountForI).

%% count_for_start_i(Nums, i) -> {Count, FinalOR}
count_for_start_i(Nums, i) ->
    % We need to track currentOR and seenValues (set of numbers in Nums[i...j])
    count_recursive(Nums, i, i, 0, #{}, 0).

%% count_recursive(Nums, i, j, current_or, seen_map, current_count) -> {count_for_i, final_or}
count_recursive(Nums, i, j, CurrentOr, SeenMap, CurrentCount) when j >= length(Nums) ->
    {CurrentCount, CurrentOr};
count_recursive(Nums, i, j, CurrentOr, SeenMap, CurrentCount) ->
    NumJ = erlang:nth(j, Nums),
    
    NewOr = CurrentOr V NumJ,
    
    % Update SeenMap. We use Map for set simulation.
    NewSeenMap = maps:insert(NumJ, true, SeenMap),
    
    IsGood = maps:is_key(NewOr, NewSeenMap),
    
    NewCount = if IsGood -> CurrentCount + 1 ; CurrentCount end,
    
    % Recurse to j+1
    count_recursive(Nums, i, j + 1, NewOr, NewSeenMap, NewCount).
```
